title: Basal Cell Carcinoma
oneLiner: Most common skin cancer, slow-growing, locally invasive, rarely metastasises. Caused by UV damage. High cure rate if treated early.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Missed melanoma:** Rapidly enlarging pigmented lesion with irregular asymmetry, border irregularity, colour variegation (red/white/blue/black), diameter >6mm. **Single finding:** Dermatoscopy shows pigment network or atypical vasculature — not arborising telangiectasias of BCC. Urgent dermatology referral (2-week wait) mandatory.
- **Aggressive squamous cell carcinoma:** Rapid growth (weeks), hyperkeratotic surface, everted/raised borders, firm to hard on palpation, possible regional lymphadenopathy. **Single finding:** Absent pearly rolled edge and telangiectasias — instead has rough, crusty, or horn-like surface. If uncertain, biopsy (punch or incisional) before assuming BCC.
- **Killer mimic rationale:** Both melanoma and SCC can metastasise and kill within months. A BCC misdiagnosis delays resection. In the first hour, pattern-recognise features that say "this is not BCC — escalate."
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal: Slow-growing (>months–years) papule, nodule, or plaque with **pearly translucent appearance**, **rolled borders**, and **arborising telangiectasias** (branching vessels visible on dermatoscopy or naked eye with good light). Common on sun-exposed areas: face, scalp, ears, neck, shoulders.
- Ulceration: Central crust or ulcer (rodent ulcer) with rolled pearly edge; may bleed spontaneously or with minor trauma.
- Variants:
  - **Nodular:** Classic dome-shaped, pearly, telangiectatic – most common.
  - **Superficial:** Pink/red scaly patch, often on trunk, mimics eczema or psoriasis.
  - **Morphoeic (sclerosing):** Scar-like, indurated white/yellow plaque, edges ill-defined – more aggressive, higher recurrence risk.
  - **Pigmented:** Brown/black colour, mimics melanoma – need dermatoscopy.
- Exam: Palpate for firmness, measure diameter, note border definition. Check regional lymph nodes only if large (>2 cm), ulcerated, or recurrent (rarely involved). Document with photograph (consent) and ruler.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Squamous cell carcinoma** (most common mimic): More rapid growth, hyperkeratotic, everted borders, no telangiectasias, may have lymphadenopathy. **Splitter:** Rolled pearly edge + telangiectasias = BCC; absent = think SCC.
- **Melanoma** (if pigmented): Asymmetry, border irregularity, colour variegation, diameter >6mm, evolving. **Splitter:** Dermatoscopy shows pigment network or globules (melanoma) vs arborising vessels (BCC). Amelanotic melanoma can mimic BCC — biopsy if uncertain.
- **Intradermal naevus:** Dome-shaped, flesh-coloured, soft, no telangiectasias, stable for years.
- **Seborrhoeic keratosis:** Stuck-on appearance, waxy, pigmented with milia-like cysts, no rolled borders or telangiectasias.
- **Bowen’s disease (SCC in situ):** Well-demarcated red scaly plaque, no rolled borders, no telangiectasias, slow growth.
- **Dermatofibroma:** Firm dermal nodule, dimple sign on lateral compression, usually on legs, brownish.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Dermatoscopy – look for **arborising telangiectasias** (branched branching vessels) and absence of pigment network. Also check for ulceration, crust, and lesion size.
- **Bloods:** Not required for diagnosis. If multiple BCCs or suspicious for Gorlin syndrome (naevoid BCC syndrome) → consider genetics referral, no specific bloods.
- **Imaging:** Not routine. Only for high-risk lesions (morphoeic, >2 cm, recurrent, near eye/nose/bone) → MRI or CT to assess deep invasion. Not first hour.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Punch biopsy** (4 mm) or **shave biopsy** for histology (gold standard) – especially if mimics considered.
  - **Incisional biopsy** for very large lesions to guide excision margins.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis is often sufficient for classic BCC, but **never rely solely on naked-eye exam** for atypical lesions. No formal scoring system. For Gorlin syndrome: multiple BCCs + palmar pits + jaw cysts + family history.
- **Never-miss test:** **Biopsy (punch or shave)** of any lesion that is not classic BCC to exclude SCC or melanoma. Omitting biopsy risks missed aggressive cancer.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** Not an ED presentation. If bleeding or infected: apply pressure, clean with saline, cover with non-adherent dressing. No urgent treatment needed. Arrange outpatient referral.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Incidental finding on admission – document size, site, photograph, and refer to dermatology (routine if classic BCC, urgent 2-week wait if atypical). No ward treatment.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Surgical excision** – first-line for most BCCs. Excision margin 4 mm for low-risk (nodular, <2 cm, non-face), 5 mm for high-risk (morphoeic, face, recurrent). Confirm histological clearance.
  - **Mohs micrographic surgery** – for high-risk sites (nose, eyelids, ears) or recurrent tumours.
  - **Non-surgical options** for low-risk superficial BCC:
    - Imiquimod 5% cream 5×/week × 6 weeks (self-applied).
    - 5-fluorouracil 5% cream twice daily × 3-4 weeks.
    - Curettage and cautery – for nodular/superficial BCC.
    - Radiotherapy – for elderly/frail patients or large lesions unsuitable for surgery.
    - Photodynamic therapy (PDT) – for superficial BCC only.
  - **Vismodegib** (oral hedgehog inhibitor) – for locally advanced or metastatic BCC not amenable to surgery/RT – specialist oncology only.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not applicable (BCC rarely requires admission). If excised as inpatient: simple wound care, no routine observations, no VTE prophylaxis unless post-op immobility.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (topical for superficial BCC):**
  - **Imiquimod 5% cream** (Aldara) – apply to lesion 5 nights per week × 6 weeks. Leave on 8 hours, wash off. Local inflammatory reaction expected. [Check local policy for exact frequency/duration.]
  - **5-Fluorouracil 5% cream** (Efudix) – apply twice daily × 3-4 weeks. Can cause intense erythema/ulceration.
- **Second-line (advanced disease):**
  - **Vismodegib** (Erivedge) – 150 mg orally once daily. Specialist initiation only. Monitor LFTs, pregnancy test (teratogenic). [Check local policy for monitoring schedule.]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Imiquimod: avoid on open wounds or infected skin.
  - 5-FU: avoid in pregnancy (P).
  - Vismodegib: teratogenic (X), must use two forms of contraception, avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Topicals – none. Vismodegib – caution in hepatic impairment; no specific dose adjustment [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Topical imiquimod/5-FU over-application** – leads to severe local inflammation, ulceration, scarring. Patient should apply only to lesion, not surrounding skin, and follow prescribed frequency exactly.
  - **Vismodegib missed pregnancy test** – teratogenic boxed warning; must confirm negative pregnancy test before initiating and monthly thereafter.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Incomplete excision (most common) – recurrence risk 15-40% if margins involved.
  - Wound infection, bleeding, or haematoma after excision.
  - Severe local reaction from topical therapy (imiquimod/5-FU) – can mimic infection.
- **Chronic:**
  - Local invasion (neglected or high-risk subtypes) – can erode into cartilage, bone, sinuses, or orbit causing functional loss.
  - Cosmetic deformity (especially face).
  - Metastasis extremely rare (<0.1%), but can occur in advanced cases.
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent overall: >95% cure rate with appropriate treatment. Recurrence rate ~5% for low-risk BCC after standard excision; higher (∼20%) for morphoeic, recurrent, or large face lesions. Metastasis risk <0.1% – mostly from large recurrent tumours. Morbidity from local invasion can be significant if untreated for years.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always photograph and measure** any skin lesion on admission – facilitates comparison and referral.
- **Refer via 2-week wait** if any clinical suspicion of SCC or melanoma – biopsy is the decider.
- **Provide sun protection counselling** – patients with one BCC have increased risk of further skin cancers; advise SPF 50+ daily and self-skin checks.

- **Do not delay biopsy** of an atypical lesion thinking it’s a BCC – you may miss an aggressive SCC or melanoma.

rawMarkdown:
## Basal Cell Carcinoma
**One-liner:** Most common skin cancer, slow-growing, locally invasive, rarely metastasises. Caused by UV damage. High cure rate if treated early.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Missed melanoma:** Rapidly enlarging pigmented lesion with irregular asymmetry, border irregularity, colour variegation (red/white/blue/black), diameter >6mm. **Single finding:** Dermatoscopy shows pigment network or atypical vasculature — not arborising telangiectasias of BCC. Urgent dermatology referral (2-week wait) mandatory.
- **Aggressive squamous cell carcinoma:** Rapid growth (weeks), hyperkeratotic surface, everted/raised borders, firm to hard on palpation, possible regional lymphadenopathy. **Single finding:** Absent pearly rolled edge and telangiectasias — instead has rough, crusty, or horn-like surface. If uncertain, biopsy (punch or incisional) before assuming BCC.
- **Killer mimic rationale:** Both melanoma and SCC can metastasise and kill within months. A BCC misdiagnosis delays resection. In the first hour, pattern-recognise features that say "this is not BCC — escalate."

### Presentation
- Cardinal: Slow-growing (>months–years) papule, nodule, or plaque with **pearly translucent appearance**, **rolled borders**, and **arborising telangiectasias** (branching vessels visible on dermatoscopy or naked eye with good light). Common on sun-exposed areas: face, scalp, ears, neck, shoulders.
- Ulceration: Central crust or ulcer (rodent ulcer) with rolled pearly edge; may bleed spontaneously or with minor trauma.
- Variants:
  - **Nodular:** Classic dome-shaped, pearly, telangiectatic – most common.
  - **Superficial:** Pink/red scaly patch, often on trunk, mimics eczema or psoriasis.
  - **Morphoeic (sclerosing):** Scar-like, indurated white/yellow plaque, edges ill-defined – more aggressive, higher recurrence risk.
  - **Pigmented:** Brown/black colour, mimics melanoma – need dermatoscopy.
- Exam: Palpate for firmness, measure diameter, note border definition. Check regional lymph nodes only if large (>2 cm), ulcerated, or recurrent (rarely involved). Document with photograph (consent) and ruler.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Squamous cell carcinoma** (most common mimic): More rapid growth, hyperkeratotic, everted borders, no telangiectasias, may have lymphadenopathy. **Splitter:** Rolled pearly edge + telangiectasias = BCC; absent = think SCC.
- **Melanoma** (if pigmented): Asymmetry, border irregularity, colour variegation, diameter >6mm, evolving. **Splitter:** Dermatoscopy shows pigment network or globules (melanoma) vs arborising vessels (BCC). Amelanotic melanoma can mimic BCC — biopsy if uncertain.
- **Intradermal naevus:** Dome-shaped, flesh-coloured, soft, no telangiectasias, stable for years.
- **Seborrhoeic keratosis:** Stuck-on appearance, waxy, pigmented with milia-like cysts, no rolled borders or telangiectasias.
- **Bowen’s disease (SCC in situ):** Well-demarcated red scaly plaque, no rolled borders, no telangiectasias, slow growth.
- **Dermatofibroma:** Firm dermal nodule, dimple sign on lateral compression, usually on legs, brownish.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Dermatoscopy – look for **arborising telangiectasias** (branched branching vessels) and absence of pigment network. Also check for ulceration, crust, and lesion size.
- **Bloods:** Not required for diagnosis. If multiple BCCs or suspicious for Gorlin syndrome (naevoid BCC syndrome) → consider genetics referral, no specific bloods.
- **Imaging:** Not routine. Only for high-risk lesions (morphoeic, >2 cm, recurrent, near eye/nose/bone) → MRI or CT to assess deep invasion. Not first hour.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Punch biopsy** (4 mm) or **shave biopsy** for histology (gold standard) – especially if mimics considered.
  - **Incisional biopsy** for very large lesions to guide excision margins.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis is often sufficient for classic BCC, but **never rely solely on naked-eye exam** for atypical lesions. No formal scoring system. For Gorlin syndrome: multiple BCCs + palmar pits + jaw cysts + family history.
- **Never-miss test:** **Biopsy (punch or shave)** of any lesion that is not classic BCC to exclude SCC or melanoma. Omitting biopsy risks missed aggressive cancer.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** Not an ED presentation. If bleeding or infected: apply pressure, clean with saline, cover with non-adherent dressing. No urgent treatment needed. Arrange outpatient referral.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Incidental finding on admission – document size, site, photograph, and refer to dermatology (routine if classic BCC, urgent 2-week wait if atypical). No ward treatment.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Surgical excision** – first-line for most BCCs. Excision margin 4 mm for low-risk (nodular, <2 cm, non-face), 5 mm for high-risk (morphoeic, face, recurrent). Confirm histological clearance.
  - **Mohs micrographic surgery** – for high-risk sites (nose, eyelids, ears) or recurrent tumours.
  - **Non-surgical options** for low-risk superficial BCC:
    - Imiquimod 5% cream 5×/week × 6 weeks (self-applied).
    - 5-fluorouracil 5% cream twice daily × 3-4 weeks.
    - Curettage and cautery – for nodular/superficial BCC.
    - Radiotherapy – for elderly/frail patients or large lesions unsuitable for surgery.
    - Photodynamic therapy (PDT) – for superficial BCC only.
  - **Vismodegib** (oral hedgehog inhibitor) – for locally advanced or metastatic BCC not amenable to surgery/RT – specialist oncology only.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not applicable (BCC rarely requires admission). If excised as inpatient: simple wound care, no routine observations, no VTE prophylaxis unless post-op immobility.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (topical for superficial BCC):**
  - **Imiquimod 5% cream** (Aldara) – apply to lesion 5 nights per week × 6 weeks. Leave on 8 hours, wash off. Local inflammatory reaction expected. [Check local policy for exact frequency/duration.]
  - **5-Fluorouracil 5% cream** (Efudix) – apply twice daily × 3-4 weeks. Can cause intense erythema/ulceration.
- **Second-line (advanced disease):**
  - **Vismodegib** (Erivedge) – 150 mg orally once daily. Specialist initiation only. Monitor LFTs, pregnancy test (teratogenic). [Check local policy for monitoring schedule.]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Imiquimod: avoid on open wounds or infected skin.
  - 5-FU: avoid in pregnancy (P).
  - Vismodegib: teratogenic (X), must use two forms of contraception, avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Topicals – none. Vismodegib – caution in hepatic impairment; no specific dose adjustment [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Topical imiquimod/5-FU over-application** – leads to severe local inflammation, ulceration, scarring. Patient should apply only to lesion, not surrounding skin, and follow prescribed frequency exactly.
  - **Vismodegib missed pregnancy test** – teratogenic boxed warning; must confirm negative pregnancy test before initiating and monthly thereafter.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Incomplete excision (most common) – recurrence risk 15-40% if margins involved.
  - Wound infection, bleeding, or haematoma after excision.
  - Severe local reaction from topical therapy (imiquimod/5-FU) – can mimic infection.
- **Chronic:**
  - Local invasion (neglected or high-risk subtypes) – can erode into cartilage, bone, sinuses, or orbit causing functional loss.
  - Cosmetic deformity (especially face).
  - Metastasis extremely rare (<0.1%), but can occur in advanced cases.

### Prognosis
- Excellent overall: >95% cure rate with appropriate treatment. Recurrence rate ~5% for low-risk BCC after standard excision; higher (∼20%) for morphoeic, recurrent, or large face lesions. Metastasis risk <0.1% – mostly from large recurrent tumours. Morbidity from local invasion can be significant if untreated for years.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always photograph and measure** any skin lesion on admission – facilitates comparison and referral.
- **Refer via 2-week wait** if any clinical suspicion of SCC or melanoma – biopsy is the decider.
- **Provide sun protection counselling** – patients with one BCC have increased risk of further skin cancers; advise SPF 50+ daily and self-skin checks.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay biopsy** of an atypical lesion thinking it’s a BCC – you may miss an aggressive SCC or melanoma.
- **Do not use topical treatments (
